title: On The Frith Of Clyde - In A Steamboat
content:
Arran! a single-crested Teneriffe,
 A St. Helena next in shape and hue,
 Varying her crowded peaks and ridges blue;
 Who but must covet a cloud-seat, or skiff
 Built for the air, or winged Hippogriff?
 That he might fly, where no one could pursue,
 From this dull Monster and her sooty crew;
 And, as a God, light on thy topmost cliff.
 Impotent wish! which reason would despise
 If the mind knew no union of extremes,
 No natural bond between the boldest schemes,
 Ambition frames, and heart-humilities.
 Beneath stern mountains many a soft vale lies,
 And lofty springs give birth to lowly streams.
meaning:
About: William Wordsworth (April 7, 1770 � April 23, 1850) was a major English romantic poet who, with Samuel Taylor Coleridge, helped launch the Romantic Age in English literature with their 1798 joint publication, Lyrical Ballads.
 Wordsworth's masterpiece is generally considered to be The Prelude, an autobiographical poem of his early years that was revised and expanded a number of times. It was never published during his lifetime, and was only given the title after his death. Up until this time it was generally known as the poem "to Coleridge". 
Wordsworth was England's Poet Laureate from 1843 until his death in 1850.
